publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 165, folios 74-75. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Dearest Mate. How very mischievous of you to begin your letter by telling the poor Avis that the nasty chesnut mare jumped about.-- I hope I shall behave better than Lady Fletcher, & wait dutifully for your return. A very kind letter from Dr Locock, which I enclose, has set the whole matter of doctors at rest in the most satisfactory manner. By his advice, I shall of course
abide precisely, & I have his enclosure for Dr Ferguson in safe keeping.-- I do not think myself at all likely to be taken ill before you return.--
I am much pleased with Green & the arrangements she is making, which promise greatly for my comfort.--

Today Mr Wharton is to come at 1/2 pst 2 o'clock with the master-in-chancery. As I find that my part of the business must be conducted without the husband's presence, I think it is just as well this opportunity is taken.--
Yesterday I went to the Hen's between

1 & 2, & was agreeably surprised to find there Mrs Dahoury, whom I brought back here with me for an hour, & have engaged to dine with us tomorrow. She was delighted with Byron, who behaved himself very well--
By the bye he is greatly charmed with his cart, especially the horse, so I think you were certainly wrong in thinking the latter should have been omitted.
I was rather sorry this morning to see a voluminous letter arrive for Hester from Charlotte, very little of which was imparted to me.-- They are certainly the Inseparables.--
Mrs. L. K continues very prosperous.
I take great care of the horses, & am in every respect a very nice Avis or Bird.--
Mr Lauzelle is going on, slowly with my teeth, & I think he will stop all further annoyance.
This is another reason I have for wishing to defer any accouchement.--
Yesterday evening it rained torrents, & I believe in the night also, but you have nice weather on the whole.--
Most affecly The Little Bird Ada
